g.data-narration: Wrapper classes in Java encapsulate primitive values into objects, enabling primitives to be used in object-oriented contexts like Collections.
g.text: Classes that encapsulate primitive values into objects, bridging primitive and reference types.
Essential for Collections, APIs requiring objects, and object-oriented programming.

g.data-narration: Java provides eight wrapper classes: Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double, Character, and Boolean - one for each primitive type.
Create wrapper objects using valueOf() methods (recommended since Java 9) or autoboxing. Avoid deprecated constructors.
g.text: ✓ Use valueOf() or autoboxing | ✗ Constructors are deprecated

g.data-narration: Autoboxing automatically converts primitives to wrapper objects. The Java compiler performs this conversion behind the scenes.
Unboxing converts wrapper objects back to primitives using xxxValue() methods or automatic unboxing during operations.
parseInt returns a primitive int while valueOf returns an Integer object. Choose based on whether you need a primitive or wrapper.
Wrapper classes provide toString(), equals(), hashCode(), and compareTo() methods for value manipulation and comparison.
Java caches Integer objects for values -128 to 127 for memory efficiency. This is a critical exam concept.
Integer objects within cache range share same reference. Use equals() for value comparison, not == which compares references.
g.text: ⚠ Always use .equals() for value comparison, not ==

g.data-narration: Java Collections can only store objects, not primitives. Wrapper classes enable storing primitives through autoboxing.
HashMap uses wrapper classes for both keys and values. Autoboxing enables primitive literals while unboxing retrieves primitives.
All numeric wrappers extend the abstract Number class, providing common conversion methods like intValue(), doubleValue().
Boolean wrapper has special behavior: only 'true' (case-insensitive) returns true. Any other string including 'false' returns false.
Key exam tips: memorize all 8 wrappers, remember cache range -128 to 127, always use equals() for value comparison.
In summary: wrapper classes convert primitives to objects, enable Collections, use autoboxing/unboxing, and require equals() for value comparison.

g.text: Byte, Short, Integer, Long, Float, Double, Character, Boolean
Type Wrappers - Essential for Java Collections & Object-Oriented Programming
